angle: AI adoption as an organizational transformation challenge
content:
AI adoption succeeds when organizations treat it as transformation, not as a side project in the lab.

At our recent KickstartAI community session, ING COO Risk Leon Dusée shared how large organizations can move from AI pilots to real, scaled impact. His message was clear: organizations reach value when they redesign how they work, lead, and learn.

🏁 Start with ownership
Many pilots stall because nobody owns the path to scale. Clear accountability and a product mindset give promising use cases a real future.

🎯 Focus on fewer, bigger bets
Organizations gain momentum when they commit to a limited number of high-impact AI use cases, instead of spreading energy across many experiments.

🧠 Invest in people
Critical thinking, adaptability, and data literacy are core skills for employees and leaders. AI systems only deliver value when teams understand, challenge, and improve them.

🏗️ Redesign end-to-end processes
The biggest gains come when organizations rethink entire processes, not just automate single tasks. That is where AI changes customer journeys and risk decisions at scale.

At KickstartAI, we bring Dutch organizations together to share these lessons across banking, aviation, retail, education, and healthcare. The Netherlands moves faster when we learn in the open.

🔗 Watch the talk by Leon Dusée: https://lnkd.in/eU8jqvT9

#AIadoption #DigitalTransformation #Netherlands #KickstartAI #ResponsibleAI

dimensions.name: tone_of_voice
dimensions.justification: The post is largely on-brand: purposeful, community-centric, and clear, with a recognizably human feel. Phrases like 'The Netherlands moves faster when we learn in the open' capture KickstartAI's collaborative spirit well. A few bullet-point lines feel slightly instructional or listicle-generic ('Critical thinking, adaptability, and data literacy are core skills'), which flattens the voice momentarily, but overall the post is consistent with the brand.

dimensions.name: language_and_style
dimensions.justification: Several violations are present. (1) 'not as a side project in the lab' uses an em-dash-free negative framing but the opening sentence 'treat it as transformation, not as a side project in the lab' is a contrastive construction also flagged under discourse_structure. (2) 'That is where AI changes customer journeys' — 'That is' opens with a vague demonstrative reference (also a discourse issue). (3) The post is approximately 230 words, within range. (4) No bold text, no em dashes, Oxford comma appears used correctly. (5) 'not just automate single tasks' is a negated construction within a sentence. (6) 'instead of spreading energy across many experiments' is a negative framing. (7) American English appears consistent. Overall there are a few style violations but no gross breaches; the post is mostly compliant.

dimensions.name: coherence_readability
dimensions.justification: The post flows logically from a framing statement, to a named speaker and context, into structured lessons, and closes with a community call-to-action and link. The bullet sections connect naturally to the opening claim. The transition from individual lessons to the broader KickstartAI mission at the end is smooth and purposeful.

dimensions.name: discourse_structure
dimensions.justification: Multiple violations are present across patterns. Three contrastive constructions appear: the opening line, a bullet-point line using 'instead of', and a clause using 'not just'. One vague 'That' reference also appears.

violations.quote: treat it as transformation, not as a side project in the lab
Organizations gain momentum when they commit to a limited number of high-impact AI use cases, instead of spreading energy across many experiments
rethink entire processes, not just automate single tasks
That is where AI changes customer journeys and risk decisions at scale

dimensions.name: specificity
dimensions.justification: The post names Leon Dusée, his role (ING COO Risk), and the KickstartAI community session, grounding it in real people and context. The closing sentence names concrete sectors (banking, aviation, retail, education, healthcare) and references the Dutch ecosystem specifically. However, the four bullet-point lessons are fairly generic best-practice advice that could apply to any organization, reducing the overall specificity score.

dimensions.name: historical_similarity
dimensions.justification: The post closely mirrors the structural template of the reference posts: an opening framing statement, a named practitioner and their key message, a bulleted breakdown of takeaways, a community/impact closing line, and a link with a call-to-action. The tone and length also align well. It avoids reusing specific content from the references while clearly belonging to the same series.

angle: From pilots to impact: focusing AI on societal value
content:
Many organizations can run an AI pilot. Far fewer can turn AI into better financial health, education, or healthcare.

During a KickstartAI session, ING COO Risk Leon Dusée shared why so many pilots stall and how leaders can change that.

Leaders who unlock AI impact tend to do three things:

🚀 Tie AI to real outcomes
Successful AI initiatives connect directly to business and societal goals, such as financial resilience for customers or safer operations in aviation.

📌 Protect capacity for scaling
Teams reserve time and budget to harden, integrate, and govern promising pilots. The scaling work is planned from day one, not added at the end.

🤝 Build cross-organizational collaboration
Organizations move faster when they share patterns, failures, and reusable building blocks across sectors. The Dutch ecosystem gains when banks, hospitals, schools, and retailers learn together.

At KickstartAI, we help organizations focus their AI efforts on a small number of high-impact use cases, and we connect leaders who want AI to deliver more than just productivity gains.

AI adoption in the Netherlands reaches the next level when impact on society becomes the guiding metric.

🔗 Full session with Leon Dusée: https://lnkd.in/eU8jqvT9

#AIforGood #SocietalImpact #AIadoption #KickstartAI #Netherlands

dimensions.name: tone_of_voice
dimensions.justification: The post is broadly purpose-driven and community-centric, referencing the Dutch ecosystem and societal impact in a way that feels recognizably KickstartAI. However, the three-bullet leadership framework ('Leaders who unlock AI impact tend to do three things') leans slightly toward a generic advisory or consulting tone rather than the humble, human, conversational voice of the reference posts. The closing line ('AI adoption in the Netherlands reaches the next level when impact on society becomes the guiding metric') is on-brand but reads a little like a slogan rather than a genuine human observation.

dimensions.name: language_and_style
dimensions.justification: Several violations are present. (1) Bold text is used throughout the emoji bullet headers ('Tie AI to real outcomes', 'Protect capacity for scaling', 'Build cross-organizational collaboration'), which is explicitly prohibited. (2) The post is approximately 220 words, which is within range. (3) The em dash rule is not violated. (4) 'from day one' is fine. (5) No Oxford comma issue detected. (6) American English is used consistently. The bold text is the clearest and most repeated violation.

dimensions.name: coherence_readability
dimensions.justification: The post moves logically from problem statement (pilots stall) to three leadership behaviors to KickstartAI's role to a closing call to action. The transition from the bullet section back to the KickstartAI paragraph is slightly abrupt — the bullets are presented as universal leader behaviors, and then KickstartAI's positioning is introduced without a bridging sentence. Overall the structure is clear and easy to follow.

dimensions.name: discourse_structure
dimensions.justification: Two violations are present. First, 'Far fewer can turn AI into better financial health, education, or healthcare' is a contrastive construction opposing 'Many organizations can run an AI pilot' against the implied negation of turning AI into impact. Second, 'not added at the end' is a negated qualifier embedded in an otherwise affirmative sentence: 'The scaling work is planned from day one, not added at the end.' No vague 'This/That' or 'from X to Y' constructions were found.

violations.quote: Many organizations can run an AI pilot. Far fewer can turn AI into better financial health, education, or healthcare.
The scaling work is planned from day one, not added at the end.

dimensions.name: specificity
dimensions.justification: The post names Leon Dusée and ING specifically, and references concrete sectors (aviation, banking, hospitals, schools, retailers). However, the three leadership behaviors are described in generic terms that could apply to any organization or any AI adoption framework — phrases like 'Tie AI to real outcomes' and 'Build cross-organizational collaboration' lack the concrete named use cases or measured outcomes (e.g., '65% food waste reduction') that make the reference posts feel grounded. The specificity is mixed rather than consistently concrete.

dimensions.name: historical_similarity
dimensions.justification: The post shares some structural DNA with the reference posts — it opens with a tension, references a named KickstartAI session and speaker, and closes with a link and hashtags. However, it diverges meaningfully from the template: the reference posts use short punchy paragraphs and emoji bullet lists to summarize interview or article takeaways, while this post constructs a standalone three-part framework with bold subheadings, giving it more of a thought-leadership listicle feel than the community-event recap or article-teaser format consistently used in the references. The bold headers especially distance it from the reference style.

angle: Leaders and teams as the real AI scaling engine
content:
AI technology keeps getting better. AI adoption still depends on people.

In our session with ING COO Risk Leon Dusée, one theme kept coming back: organizations scale AI when leaders and teams grow with the technology.

👩‍💼 Leadership sets the pace
Leaders who treat AI as a core capability make clear choices on focus, governance, and risk. They encourage experimentation while still setting firm boundaries.

🧩 Teams own the change
Multidisciplinary teams that include domain experts, data scientists, engineers, and risk specialists bridge the gap between models and real operations.

🧠 Skills go beyond coding
Critical thinking, understanding uncertainty, and working with data-driven decisions are just as important as model-building skills.

When organizations redesign processes around these capabilities, AI starts to reshape entire workflows, not only optimize individual tasks. That is where real value emerges for customers and for society.

KickstartAI exists to support this shift in the Dutch ecosystem. We create spaces where practitioners share how they handle ownership, education, and collaboration, so that every new use case scales faster and safer than the last.

🔗 Learn from Leon Dusée's experience: https://lnkd.in/eU8jqvT9

#Leadership #AIinPractice #AIadoption #KickstartAI #Netherlands

dimensions.name: tone_of_voice
dimensions.justification: The post is largely on-brand — purposeful, community-centric, and human — with lines like 'KickstartAI exists to support this shift' and 'so that every new use case scales faster and safer than the last.' However, the bullet-point headers ('Leadership sets the pace,' 'Teams own the change,' 'Skills go beyond coding') feel slightly listicle-corporate rather than conversational, and the opening two-sentence contrast reads a touch aphoristic rather than naturally approachable.

dimensions.name: language_and_style
dimensions.justification: Several violations are present. (1) Bold text is used throughout: '👩‍💼 Leadership sets the pace', '🧩 Teams own the change', '🧠 Skills go beyond coding' — bold is prohibited. (2) 'not only optimize individual tasks' is part of a longer construction that bends toward the prohibited style. (3) The post is approximately 190 words, which is within the 150–250 range — no violation there. (4) No em dashes detected. (5) American English appears consistent. (6) The Oxford comma is used correctly ('domain experts, data scientists, engineers, and risk specialists'). Overall, the bold-text violations alone constitute a clear, repeated breach of the style rules.

dimensions.name: coherence_readability
dimensions.justification: The post moves logically from the session context, through three capability pillars, to an organizational-level payoff, and then to KickstartAI's role. The three bullet sections connect well to the closing paragraph. The transition from the bullet list back to prose ('When organizations redesign processes…') is slightly abrupt but not confusing.

dimensions.name: discourse_structure
dimensions.justification: Two clear violations are present. First, 'AI starts to reshape entire workflows, not only optimize individual tasks' is a contrastive construction ('not only … but'). Second, 'so that every new use case scales faster and safer than the last' is clean. However, 'Leaders who treat AI as a core capability make clear choices on focus, governance, and risk. They encourage experimentation while still setting firm boundaries' is fine. The contrastive pattern and one implicit from-to idea in the opening ('AI technology keeps getting better. AI adoption still depends on people') reads as a contrastive framing pairing.

violations.quote: AI starts to reshape entire workflows, not only optimize individual tasks.
AI technology keeps getting better. AI adoption still depends on people.

dimensions.name: specificity
dimensions.justification: The post names Leon Dusée and ING COO Risk, and links to a specific article, which grounds it. However, the three bullet sections ('Leadership sets the pace,' 'Teams own the change,' 'Skills go beyond coding') contain almost entirely generic claims that could apply to any AI adoption effort at any organization — no concrete examples, no named outcomes, no specific ING or KickstartAI use cases are cited to substantiate the claims.

dimensions.name: historical_similarity
dimensions.justification: The post borrows the reference-to-an-article structure used in Reference 1 (linking to the Leon Dusée piece), and shares the community-purpose closing tone of all three references. However, the historical posts avoid bold-text headers and structured bullet sections in favor of flowing prose or emoji-prefixed short lines without bolding. The heavy use of bold subheadings gives this post a 'listicle' structure that is not present in any reference post, reducing template fidelity.
